note_data.title: Psychology and abnormality
keyPoints: **Abnormality** can be defined using statistical infrequency, deviation from social norms, failure to function adequately, or deviation from ideal mental health
**Statistical infrequency** defines abnormal behaviour as occurring in less than 5% of the population on a normal distribution curve
**Deviation from social norms** defines abnormality as behaviour that violates society's accepted standards and expectations
**DSM-5** is a classification system that uses categorical diagnosis with specific criteria for mental disorders
**Schizophrenia** is characterized by positive symptoms (hallucinations, delusions) and negative symptoms (avolition, speech poverty)
**Major depressive disorder** requires at least 5 symptoms including depressed mood or anhedonia for minimum 2 weeks
**Cognitive Behavioural Therapy (CBT)** treats abnormality by changing negative thought patterns and maladaptive behaviours
**Drug therapy** for schizophrenia uses antipsychotics that block dopamine receptors to reduce positive symptoms
**Reliability** in diagnosis means consistency of results across time and between different clinicians
**Validity** in diagnosis means the classification system measures what it claims to measure

definitions.definition: Behaviour that deviates from statistical norms, social norms, interferes with daily functioning, or represents absence of positive mental health characteristics
A mental disorder characterized by disruptions in thought processes, perceptions, emotional responsiveness and social interactions
A mood disorder involving persistent feelings of sadness, hopelessness and loss of interest in activities for at least two weeks
A psychological treatment that focuses on changing negative thought patterns and behaviours that maintain mental disorders
Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, fifth edition - a classification system providing criteria for diagnosing mental disorders

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give strengths AND weaknesses with evidence/examples
Identify similarities AND differences between concepts

modelAnswer.question: Explain two limitations of using deviation from social norms to define abnormality. [4 marks]
modelAnswer.answer: One limitation is cultural relativism because what is considered normal varies between cultures, for example hearing voices may be seen as spiritual communication in some Pakistani communities but abnormal in Western cultures. Another limitation is that social norms change over time because behaviours once considered abnormal may become acceptable, for example homosexuality was classified as a mental disorder but is now accepted in many societies.
markBreakdown: Mark 2: Example of cultural difference given
Mark 4: Example of changing norms provided

commonMistakes: Never say 'mental illness' without defining it - always use specific diagnostic criteria
Students often confuse positive and negative symptoms of schizophrenia - positive symptoms ADD experiences, negative symptoms REMOVE normal functioning
Don't just list symptoms - always explain how they meet diagnostic criteria
Never say CBT 'cures' disorders - it 'treats' or 'reduces symptoms'
Students confuse reliability with validity - reliability is consistency, validity is accuracy
examinerTips: This topic appears in every Paper 2 - focus on definitions of abnormality
Always give specific examples when evaluating cultural differences
Use real diagnostic criteria from DSM-5 for full marks
Compare biological vs psychological treatments for evaluation marks
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Normal distribution curve showing statistical infrequency with 95% of population in middle section and 2.5% at each extreme representing abnormal behaviour
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to explain two different definitions of abnormality and give examples of each
This topic appeared in O/N 2022 P1 Q2 (4 marks) — students had to describe the statistical infrequency definition and identify one limitation
This topic was examined in M/J 2022 P2 Q3 (8 marks) — candidates were required to compare deviation from social norms with failure to function adequately as definitions of abnormality
This topic featured in O/N 2023 P1 Q1 (3 marks) — students needed to explain what is meant by 'deviation from ideal mental health'
This topic was tested in M/J 2021 P2 Q2 (5 marks) — they asked for an evaluation of using cultural relativism when defining abnormality

workedExample.question: Explain what is meant by 'statistical infrequency' as a definition of abnormality. Give one example. (4 marks)
steps: Step 1: Define statistical infrequency clearly - behaviors/characteristics that are numerically rare in the population
Step 2: Explain the mechanism - uses mathematical cut-off points, typically 2 standard deviations from the mean
Step 3: Provide a clear psychological example with specific details
Step 4: Ensure the example demonstrates the numerical rarity aspect
workedExample.answer: Statistical infrequency defines abnormality as any behavior or characteristic that is numerically rare or uncommon in the general population. This approach uses statistical data to identify what is 'normal' by looking at the frequency distribution of behaviors, with abnormality typically defined as anything occurring in less than 5% of the population (2 standard deviations from the mean). An example would be intellectual disability, where an IQ score below 70 occurs in approximately 2% of the population and is therefore considered statistically abnormal.
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for definition of statistical infrequency, 1 mark for reference to numerical rarity/percentages, 1 mark for appropriate example, 1 mark for explaining why the example demonstrates statistical infrequency

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused the different definitions of abnormality and mixed up examples' — always clearly state which definition you are discussing and ensure examples match that specific definition

Examiner Report 2022: 'Students often failed to provide evaluative points when asked to assess definitions' — remember to discuss limitations such as cultural relativism, positive abnormalities, and context dependency

Examiner Report 2023: 'Candidates frequently gave vague examples without explaining how they relate to the definition' — always explicitly link your examples back to the key features of the definition you are explaining
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw a normal distribution curve showing statistical infrequency. The diagram should include: a bell curve with the mean in the center, standard deviation markers at -2, -1, +1, +2, shaded areas at both extremes (beyond ±2 SD) labeled as 'abnormal', the central area labeled as 'normal', percentages marked (2.5% in each tail, 95% in center), and specific examples plotted (e.g., IQ scores of 70 and 130 at the cut-off points). Include clear labels for the x-axis (behavior/trait being measured) and y-axis (frequency/number of people).
note_data.quickRecap: Abnormality can be defined in four main ways: statistical infrequency (numerically rare behaviors), deviation from social norms (breaking cultural rules), failure to function adequately (inability to cope with daily life), and deviation from ideal mental health (lacking optimal psychological wellbeing). Each definition has strengths and limitations, particularly regarding cultural differences and context. Statistical infrequency uses mathematical cut-offs, while social norms vary across cultures and time periods.
